Understanding event propagation — the order in which elements receive events — is essential for building correct handlers, especially when using event delegation.
The modern API for attaching event handlers. It is preferred over the older

Milliseconds since page load when the event occurred
When an event fires on an element, the browser first runs capture-phase listeners from the document root
to the target, then runs bubble-phase listeners from the target
back to the root. Most events bubble; a few (like

to determine which child was clicked. This is efficient for dynamic lists and reduces memory usage.

. This is useful for cross-component communication when a full event bus is too heavy.

API unifies mouse, touch, and pen input under a single event family. It replaces the need to handle
fires a callback only when an element enters or leaves the viewport. It runs asynchronously off the main thread.
In Angular, test event handling through
— dispatch events on the debug element and assert the resulting state.
DOM events are the primary mechanism for user interaction in the browser. Key points:
addEventListener(type, handler, options) is the standard API; use { once: true } for one-shot listeners
Events propagate in two phases: capture (top-down) then bubble (bottom-up); bubble phase is the default
e.target is the originating element; e.currentTarget is the element with the listener
Event delegation: one listener on a parent handles all matching children via e.target.closest()
preventDefault() cancels browser defaults (form submit, link navigation); stopPropagation() stops further propagation
CustomEvent lets you dispatch typed, payloaded events on any DOM element
In Angular: use (event) template bindings or @HostListener; manually-registered listeners must be removed in ngOnDestroy
